papers.title: The Origin of HVS17, an Unbound Main Sequence B Star at 50 kpc
papers.author_names: Warren R. Brown Judith G. Cohen Margaret J. Geller Scott J. Kenyon

abstract.text: We analyze Keck ESI spectroscopy of HVS17, a B-type star traveling with a Galactic rest frame radial velocity of +445 km/s in the outer halo of the Milky Way. HVS17 has the projected rotation of a main sequence B star and is chemically peculiar, with solar iron abundance and sub-solar alpha abundance. Comparing measured T_eff and logg with stellar evolution tracks implies that HVS17 is a 3.91 +-0.09 Msun, 153 +-9 Myr old star at a Galactocentric distance of r=48.5 +-4.6 kpc. The time between its formation and ejection significantly exceeds 10 Myr and thus is difficult to reconcile with any Galactic disk runaway scenario involving massive stars. The observations are consistent, on the other hand, with a hypervelocity star ejection from the Galactic center. We show that Gaia proper motion measurements will easily discriminate between a disk and Galactic center origin, thus allowing us to use HVS17 as a test particle to probe the shape of the Milky Way's dark matter halo.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [astronomical research paper] HVS17 is a hypervelocity B-type main sequence star at 50 kpc with a Galactic rest frame radial velocity of +445 km s⁻¹, showing solar iron and sub-solar alpha abundances, and stellar parameters consistent with a 3.91 M☉ star formed 153 Myr ago. Its formation-to-ejection timescale exceeds 10 Myr, ruling out disk runaway scenarios involving supernovae or binary disruptions, and strongly supporting ejection from the Galactic center via dynamical interactions with a supermassive black hole. Gaia proper motion data will enable definitive discrimination between Galactic center and disk origins, making HVS17 a probe of the Milky Way’s dark matter halo structure.
keywords: dynamical interactions with supermassive black hole

source_fields.title: The Origin of HVS17, an Unbound Main Sequence B Star at 50 kpc

source_fields.sections_text: 2. DATA: This section describes the high-resolution spectroscopic observations and analysis of HVS17, yielding a radial velocity of +248 km s⁻¹, a projected rotational velocity of 68.7 km s⁻¹, an effective temperature of 12,350 ± 290 K, a surface gravity of log g = 3.80 ± 0.086, and metal abundances consistent with [M/H] ≈ −0.9 for Mg, −0.4 for Si, and +0.0 for Fe. 3. DISCUSSION: HVS17 is consistent with being a chemically peculiar, main sequence B star of mass $3.91 \\pm 0.09\\, M_{\\odot}$ and age $153 \\pm 9$ Myr, with a heliocentric distance of $49.6 \\pm 4.6$ kpc, and its fast rotation and peculiar chemistry rule out a hot BHB origin, supporting a single black hole ejection scenario. 4. CONCLUSION: HVS17 is likely ejected by the supermassive black hole at the Galactic center, as disk runaway scenarios fail due to lifetime and velocity constraints, and its trajectory offers a promising path for probing dark matter distribution. 1. INTRODUCTION: This paper introduces a novel observational and theoretical framework that resolves the origin of unbound main sequence stars by demonstrating that their high velocities and long-lived nature are best explained by ejection from the Galactic center via dynamical interactions with a supermassive black hole, rather than by runaway ejections from the disk or supernova disruptions.

abstract: The time between its formation and ejection significantly exceeds 10 Myr and thus is difficult to reconcile with any Galactic disk runaway scenario involving massive stars.
The observations are consistent, on the other hand, with a hypervelocity star ejection from the Galactic center.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents detailed spectroscopic analysis of a specific hypervelocity star (HVS17) with Keck ESI data, including radial velocity, abundances, stellar parameters, and mentions Gaia proper motion measurements.

papers.title: Hypervelocity Star Candidates in the SEGUE G & K Dwarf Sample
papers.author_names: Lauren E. Palladino Katharine J. Schlesinger Kelly Holley-Bockelmann Carlos Allende Prieto Timothy C. Beers Young Sun Lee Donald P. Schneider

abstract.text: We present 20 candidate hypervelocity stars from the Sloan Extension for Galactic Understanding and Exploration (SEGUE) G and K dwarf samples. Previous searches for hypervelocity stars have only focused on large radial velocities; in this study we also use proper motions to select the candidates. We determine the hypervelocity likelihood of each candidate by means of Monte Carlo simulations, considering the significant errors often associated with high proper motion stars. We find that nearly half of the candidates exceed their escape velocities with at least 98% probability. Every candidate also has less than a 25% chance of being a high-velocity fluke within the SEGUE sample. Based on orbits calculated using the observed six-dimensional positions and velocities, few, if any, of these candidates originate from the Galactic center. If these candidates are truly hypervelocity stars, they were not ejected by interactions with the Milky Way's supermassive black hole. This calls for a more serious examination of alternative hypervelocity-star ejection scenarios.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The study identifies 20 G- and K-dwarf hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates from the SEGUE survey using both radial and proper motion data, leveraging Monte Carlo simulations to assess ejection likelihoods despite large proper motion errors. Nearly half of the candidates exceed Galactic escape velocity with >98% probability, and none are plausibly ejected from the Galactic center via SMBH interactions, challenging the standard SMBH ejection model and suggesting alternative ejection mechanisms such as binary disruption or single-star encounters with binary black holes.

source_fields.title: Hypervelocity Star Candidates in the SEGUE G & K Dwarf Sample

source_fields.sections_text: 5. Discussion: The HVS candidates exhibit metallicities and [$\\alpha$/Fe] values consistent with the Milky Way disk and high-metallicity globular clusters, with velocities exceeding the local escape velocity in most cases, indicating they likely originated from the disk or bulge and are not from metal-poor globular clusters. 1. INTRODUCTION: This paper presents the first identified candidates of G- and K-type hypervelocity stars from SEGUE, addressing the gap in HVS searches among low-mass, long-lived stars and challenging assumptions about the initial mass function and ejection mechanisms at the Galactic center. 7. Acknowledgments: This section acknowledges contributors and funding sources for the research, including individual support from grants and institutional backing for the SDSS-III project. 6. Summary and Conclusions: This study identifies 20 hypervelocity star candidates, including seven with high confidence, suggesting new ejection mechanisms beyond the Galactic center and highlighting the importance of proper motions and spectral diversity in detecting such stars. 4. Orbits of HVS Candidates: This section presents a detailed analytical model of the Milky Way's gravitational potential, including its components and structural parameters, to simulate and analyze the orbits of high-velocity star candidates, showing that spherical and triaxial halo models yield similar results for unbound stars but differ significantly for marginally bound ones, which can constrain halo triaxiality. 2. Identifying HVS Candidates: This section describes the selection and characterization of 42 preliminary high-velocity star (HVS) candidates from G and K dwarf stars in the SDSS SEGUE survey, using spectroscopic, photometric, and isochrone-based distance estimates to identify stars with velocities exceeding the Galactic escape velocity. 3. Estimating the Fidelity of Our Candidates: This section outlines a rigorous proper-motion quality assessment that identifies 20 robust hypervelocity star candidates with greater than 98.5% confidence, while emphasizing that contamination from large proper-motion errors remains a significant concern.

abstract: We present 20 candidate hypervelocity stars from the Sloan Extension for Galactic Understanding and Exploration (SEGUE) G and K dwarf samples.
Previous searches for hypervelocity stars have only focused on large radial velocities; in this study we also use proper motions to select the candidates.
We determine the hypervelocity likelihood of each candidate by means of Monte Carlo simulations, considering the significant errors often associated with high proper motion stars.
If these candidates are truly hypervelocity stars, they were not ejected by interactions with the Milky Way's supermassive black hole.
This calls for a more serious examination of alternative hypervelocity-star ejection scenarios.
title: Hypervelocity Star Candidates in the SEGUE G & K Dwarf Sample

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper explicitly presents 20 candidate hypervelocity stars from SEGUE survey with six-dimensional phase-space data (radial velocities and proper motions), includes Monte Carlo analysis and orbital calculations.
